---
title: "04-Discussion"
Author: "Mikkel E. Steentoft"
format: html
editor: visual
theme: cosmo
bibliography: references.bib
csl: acta-diabetologica.csl
---

In this study, we used k-means clustering to identify three subgroups with distinct responses on glucose, insulin, GLP-1, GIP and glucagon derived from the three point oral glucose tolerance test. Importantly, the clustering revealed phenotypes not only with different glycemic status, but also distinct incretin and glucagon dynamics, suggesting that postprandial regulation is heterogeneous beyond glucose alone.

### Hormonal responses during the OGTT

Cluster 3 was the most metabolically compromised and adipose subgroup, with insulin resistance and compensatory hyperinsulinemia, dysglycemia, highest VAT, BMI and body fat percentage. This group was also the oldest and had the highest proportion of smokers and individuals with type 2 diabetes, IFG+IGT and IGT. Cluster 3 had elevated fasting insulin levels (53.6 pmol/L) and a 2-hour insulin level of 387.2 pmol/L, alongside the highest 2hPG of 9.1 mmol/L. This indicates that insulin resistance has led to compensatory hyperinsulinemia, suggesting some degree of preserved beta-cell function, but that the hypersecretion of insulin is not sufficient to compensate entirely for the insulin resistance [@defronzo2004]. Glucagon regulation was also impaired in cluster 3, with an early (0-30 minutes) increase in glucagon, despite an adequate late glucagon suppression. Low or negative glucagon suppression results in continued hepatic glucose output in the early postprandial period, contributing to the elevated glucose levels. This is consistent with prior evidence linking impaired early glucagon suppression to insulin resistance and progression to type 2 diabetes [@færch2016; @wagner2017; @knop2007]. Importantly, cluster 3 had almost similar overall glucagon suppression to cluster 1 (45.4% vs 47%), despite having very different early and late patterns. This suggests that while the α-cells of cluster 3 are resistant to early-phase inhibition by insulin, the compensatory insulin secretion observed by 120 minutes eventually results in glucagon suppression. This may reflect that glucagon suppression in cluster 3 is achieved through a delayed and compensatory mechanism, rather than the efficient and early suppression in cluster 1 [@gromada2007]. This complexity is further illustrated by the findings in cluster 2. Despite cluster 2 having the highest insulin sensitivity, this group had lower glucagon suppression at all intervals compared to cluster 1, but this did not result in elevated glucose levels. The finding of nonsuppressed glucagon<sub>120</sub> among insulin-sensitive individuals is in line with previous findings [@færch2016; @wagner2017], which suggests that cluster 2's low glucagon suppression<sub>120</sub> reflects a metabolically healthy phenotype rather than a pathological one. Interestingly, cluster 1 and 2 had relatively similar insulin sensitivity and proportions of individuals with NGT, but very different incretin secretions, with cluster 1 having the lowest incretin secretion across all clusters. The preserved glucagon suppression in cluster 1, despite low incretin levels, suggests that their insulin secretion was sufficient to inhibit glucagon secretion under current conditions [@gromada2007]. While incretins are typically important regulators of both insulin and glucagon secretion, these mechanisms are known to be glucose-dependent [@christensen2011], indicating that in insulin-sensitive individuals, adequate glucagon suppression can be maintained even when incretin secretion is reduced. However, the observation of lower incretin secretion in cluster 1 also raises the question of whether this reflects a subgroup at higher risk of developing type 2 diabetes, as lower levels of GLP-1 previously have been suggested to already be present in individuals with prediabetes within this cohort [@færch2015]. Conversely, cluster 3 exhibits higher incretin secretion than cluster 1, indicating that the interplay between impaired glucose metabolism, obesity and incretin secretion is complex. This is underscored by several studies, showing both decreased [@laakso2008; @muscelli2006; @toft-nielsen2001], normal [@vollmer2008; @ahren1997] and increased [@chong2022; @færch2008] incretin secretion among individuals with prediabetes and diabetes compared to normoglycemic individuals. The higher GLP-1 secretion in cluster 3 may reflect a compensatory mechanism to maintain sufficient insulin secretion as suggested previously [@færch2008]. The elevated GIP may also partly reflect adiposity-related GIP secretion, consistent with prior evidence linking obesity to higher fasting and postprandial GIP levels [@vilsbøll2003]. In summary, these findings highlight that the hormonal responses of dysglycemia differ between subgroups, providing nuance to the endocrine heterogeneity in prediabetes.

### Associations with body fat distribution

Beyond the dynamic hormonal responses, the three clusters differed in their associations with body fat distribution. The insulin-resistant profile of cluster 3 was associated with higher VAT, SAT, VAT/SAT ratio and body fat percentage, in line with previous findings from the ADDITION-PRO cohort [@philipsen2015], in which higher VAT, SAT, VAT/SAT ratio and body fat percentage was associated with lower insulin sensitivity, higher FPG and 2hPG. Furthermore excess VAT has been associated with all categories of impaired glucose metabolism, including iIFG, iIGT, IFG+IGT and newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes [@borel2015]. Consistent with this, VAT and the VAT/SAT ratio have been shown to increase progressively from NGT through prediabetes to type 2 diabetes [@maskarinec2022].

### Metabolic heterogeneity in prediabetes
